title: Getting Started with predictset
text: Machine learning models produce point predictions - single number regression, single class classification. practice, need know uncertain prediction . Conformal prediction wraps model layer calibrated uncertainty quantification. Given target coverage level (say 90%), produces: Prediction intervals (regression) guaranteed contain true value least 90% time Prediction sets (classification) guaranteed contain true class least 90% time key property: guarantee holds finite samples, without distributional assumptions. requirement calibration test data exchangeable (roughly: drawn distribution). predictset implements main conformal methods recent literature lightweight, model-agnostic R package two dependencies (cli stats).

title: Getting Started with predictset
text: Split conformal prediction linear model using formula shorthand: alpha parameter controls miscoverage rate: alpha = 0.10 targets 90% coverage.
code: library(predictset)  set.seed(42) n <- 500 x <- matrix(rnorm(n * 5), ncol = 5) y <- x[, 1] * 2 + x[, 2] + rnorm(n) x_new <- matrix(rnorm(100 * 5), ncol = 5)  result <- conformal_split(x, y, model = y ~ ., x_new = x_new, alpha = 0.10) print(result) #>  #> ── Conformal Prediction Intervals (Split Conformal) ──────────────────────────── #> • Coverage target: "90%" #> • Training: 250 | Calibration: 250 | Predictions: 100 #> • Conformal quantile: 1.876 #> • Median interval width: 3.7519

title: Getting Started with predictset
text: three ways specify model: 1. Formula shorthand (fits lm internally): 2. Fitted model (auto-detected lm, glm, ranger): 3. Custom model via make_model() (works anything):
code: result <- conformal_split(x, y, model = y ~ ., x_new = x_new) fit <- lm(y ~ ., data = data.frame(y = y, x)) result <- conformal_split(x, y, model = fit, x_new = x_new) rf <- make_model(   train_fun = function(x, y) {     ranger::ranger(y ~ ., data = data.frame(y = y, x))   },   predict_fun = function(object, x_new) {     predict(object, data = as.data.frame(x_new))$predictions   },   type = "regression" )  result <- conformal_split(x, y, model = rf, x_new = x_new)

title: Getting Started with predictset
text: APS produces prediction sets adapt difficulty observation. Easy cases get small sets (often single class), ambiguous cases get larger sets.
code: set.seed(42) n <- 600 x <- matrix(rnorm(n * 4), ncol = 4) y <- factor(ifelse(x[, 1] > 0.5, "A", ifelse(x[, 2] > 0, "B", "C"))) x_new <- matrix(rnorm(100 * 4), ncol = 4)  clf <- make_model(   train_fun = function(x, y) {     ranger::ranger(y ~ ., data = data.frame(y = y, x), probability = TRUE)   },   predict_fun = function(object, x_new) {     predict(object, data = as.data.frame(x_new))$predictions   },   type = "classification" )  result <- conformal_aps(x, y, model = clf, x_new = x_new, alpha = 0.10) print(result)  # Most predictions are a single class; ambiguous ones include 2-3 table(set_size(result))

title: Getting Started with predictset
text: Standard conformal guarantees marginal coverage (across test points), coverage can vary across subgroups. Mondrian conformal computes separate quantile group, guaranteeing coverage within subgroup. critical fairness regulatory compliance.
code: set.seed(42) n <- 600 x <- matrix(rnorm(n * 3), ncol = 3) groups <- factor(ifelse(x[, 1] > 0, "high", "low")) y <- x[, 1] * 2 + ifelse(groups == "high", 3, 0.5) * rnorm(n) x_new <- matrix(rnorm(200 * 3), ncol = 3) groups_new <- factor(ifelse(x_new[, 1] > 0, "high", "low"))  result <- conformal_mondrian(x, y, model = y ~ ., x_new = x_new,                               groups = groups, groups_new = groups_new)  # Check per-group coverage y_new <- x_new[, 1] * 2 + ifelse(groups_new == "high", 3, 0.5) * rnorm(200) coverage_by_group(result, y_new, groups_new) #>   group  coverage   n target #> 1  high 0.8672566 113    0.9 #> 2   low 0.8850575  87    0.9

code: set.seed(42) n <- 500 x <- matrix(rnorm(n * 3), ncol = 3) y <- x[, 1] * 2 + rnorm(n) x_new <- matrix(rnorm(200 * 3), ncol = 3) y_new <- x_new[, 1] * 2 + rnorm(200)  comp <- conformal_compare(x, y, model = y ~ ., x_new = x_new, y_new = y_new,                            methods = c("split", "cv", "jackknife")) print(comp) #>  #> ── Conformal Method Comparison ───────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> • split: coverage = 0.89, mean width = 3.388, time = 0.001s #> • cv: coverage = 0.89, mean width = 3.308, time = 0.039s #> • jackknife: coverage = 0.89, mean width = 3.335, time = 34.389s

code: library(predictset)  set.seed(42) n <- 500 x <- matrix(rnorm(n * 5), ncol = 5) y <- x[, 1] * 2 + x[, 2] + rnorm(n) x_new <- matrix(rnorm(100 * 5), ncol = 5) y_new <- x_new[, 1] * 2 + x_new[, 2] + rnorm(100)  # true values (for evaluation)  # Fit conformal intervals result <- conformal_split(x, y, model = y ~ ., x_new = x_new, alpha = 0.10) print(result) #> ── Conformal Prediction Intervals (Split Conformal) ── #> • Coverage target: "90%" #> • Training: 250 | Calibration: 250 | Predictions: 100 #> • Conformal quantile: 1.876 #> • Median interval width: 3.7519  # Extract intervals as a data frame head(data.frame(pred = result$pred, lower = result$lower, upper = result$upper))  # Verify coverage: should be ≥ 90% coverage(result, y_new)  # Predict on new data later (fit once, predict many times) future_data <- matrix(rnorm(50 * 5), ncol = 5) predict(result, newdata = future_data)

title: Conformal Prediction and Uncertainty Quantification
code: set.seed(42) n <- 400 x <- matrix(rnorm(n * 4), ncol = 4) y <- factor(ifelse(x[, 1] + x[, 2] > 0, "A", "B")) x_new <- matrix(rnorm(50 * 4), ncol = 4)  clf <- make_model(   train_fun = function(x, y) glm(y ~ ., data = data.frame(y = y, x),                                   family = "binomial"),   predict_fun = function(object, x_new) {     df <- as.data.frame(x_new)     names(df) <- paste0("X", seq_len(ncol(x_new)))     p <- predict(object, newdata = df, type = "response")     cbind(A = 1 - p, B = p)   },   type = "classification" )  result <- conformal_aps(x, y, model = clf, x_new = x_new, alpha = 0.10) result$sets[[1]]   # prediction set for first observation: e.g. c("A") result$sets[[10]]  # ambiguous case might include: c("A", "B") table(set_size(result))  # distribution of set sizes

title: Conformal Prediction and Uncertainty Quantification
text: Jackknife+ uses leave-one-refitting produce prediction intervals without splitting data. gives tighter intervals split conformal (uses data training calibration) cost refitting model n times.
code: set.seed(42) n <- 200 x <- matrix(rnorm(n * 3), ncol = 3) y <- x[, 1]^2 + x[, 2] + rnorm(n, sd = 0.5) x_new <- matrix(rnorm(50 * 3), ncol = 3)  rf <- make_model(   train_fun = function(x, y) {     ranger::ranger(y ~ ., data = data.frame(y = y, x))   },   predict_fun = function(object, x_new) {     predict(object, data = as.data.frame(x_new))$predictions   },   type = "regression" )  result <- conformal_jackknife(x, y, model = rf, x_new = x_new, alpha = 0.10) print(result) plot(result)

title: Conformal Prediction and Uncertainty Quantification
text: noise varies across input space (e.g. predictions uncertain extreme values), standard conformal intervals wide low-noise regions narrow high-noise ones. Normalised conformal scoring fixes scaling residuals local estimate variability.
code: set.seed(42) n <- 500 x <- matrix(runif(n, 0, 10), ncol = 1) y <- sin(x[, 1]) + rnorm(n, sd = 0.1 + 0.3 * x[, 1])  # noise grows with x x_new <- matrix(seq(0, 10, length.out = 100), ncol = 1)  result <- conformal_split(   x, y, model = y ~ ., x_new = x_new, alpha = 0.10,   score_type = "normalized" )  # Intervals are narrower near x = 0, wider near x = 10 plot(result)

title: Conformal Prediction and Uncertainty Quantification
text: Standard conformal prediction guarantees marginal coverage (across test points), coverage can vary wildly across subgroups. Mondrian conformal computes separate quantile group, guaranteeing coverage within subgroup. critical fairness regulatory compliance. R package CRAN implements Mondrian conformal prediction.
code: set.seed(42) n <- 600 x <- matrix(rnorm(n * 3), ncol = 3) groups <- factor(ifelse(x[, 1] > 0, "high", "low")) y <- x[, 1] * 2 + ifelse(groups == "high", 3, 0.5) * rnorm(n) x_new <- matrix(rnorm(200 * 3), ncol = 3) groups_new <- factor(ifelse(x_new[, 1] > 0, "high", "low"))  result <- conformal_mondrian(x, y, model = y ~ ., x_new = x_new,                               groups = groups, groups_new = groups_new) print(result)  # Check per-group coverage y_new <- x_new[, 1] * 2 + ifelse(groups_new == "high", 3, 0.5) * rnorm(200) coverage_by_group(result, y_new, groups_new)

title: Conformal Prediction and Uncertainty Quantification
text: test distribution differs training distribution (covariate shift), standard conformal coverage guarantees break . Weighted conformal prediction uses importance weights correct shift.
code: set.seed(42) n <- 500 x <- matrix(rnorm(n * 3), ncol = 3) y <- x[, 1] * 2 + rnorm(n) x_new <- matrix(rnorm(100 * 3, mean = 1), ncol = 3)  # shifted test data  # Importance weights (likelihood ratio of test vs training distributions) weights <- dnorm(x[, 1], mean = 1) / dnorm(x[, 1], mean = 0)  result <- conformal_weighted(x, y, model = y ~ ., x_new = x_new,                               weights = weights) print(result)

title: Conformal Prediction and Uncertainty Quantification
text: CQR combines conformal prediction quantile regression produce intervals naturally adapt heteroscedasticity. Instead fitting model mean adding symmetric bands, CQR fits models lower upper quantiles adjusts guarantee coverage.
code: set.seed(42) n <- 500 x <- matrix(rnorm(n * 3), ncol = 3) y <- x[, 1] + x[, 2]^2 + rnorm(n, sd = 0.5 + abs(x[, 1])) x_new <- matrix(rnorm(100 * 3), ncol = 3)  # In practice, use quantile regression (e.g. quantreg::rq). # Here we approximate with shifted linear models for illustration. model_lo <- make_model(   train_fun = function(x, y) lm(y ~ ., data = data.frame(y = y, x)),   predict_fun = function(obj, x_new) predict(obj, newdata = as.data.frame(x_new)) - 1.5,   type = "regression" ) model_hi <- make_model(   train_fun = function(x, y) lm(y ~ ., data = data.frame(y = y, x)),   predict_fun = function(obj, x_new) predict(obj, newdata = as.data.frame(x_new)) + 1.5,   type = "regression" )  result <- conformal_cqr(x, y, model_lo, model_hi, x_new = x_new, alpha = 0.10) print(result) plot(result)

title: Conformal Prediction and Uncertainty Quantification
text: Conformal prediction introduced Vovk, Gammerman, Shafer early 2000s. key insight calibration test data exchangeable (.e. joint distribution invariant permutation), conformal p-value uniformly distributed, gives exact finite-sample coverage guarantee. Unlike bootstrap Bayesian intervals, guarantee holds regardless model misspecification. recent explosion interest conformal prediction driven several methodological advances make practical modern machine learning: Vovk, Gammerman, Shafer (2005). Algorithmic Learning Random World. Springer. foundational book introducing conformal prediction. Lei, G’Sell, Rinaldo, Tibshirani, Wasserman (2018). Distribution-free predictive inference regression. Journal American Statistical Association, 113(523), 1094–1111. Formalises split conformal prediction. Barber, Candes, Ramdas, Tibshirani (2021). Predictive inference Jackknife+. Annals Statistics, 49(1), 486–507. Introduces Jackknife+ CV+, avoid efficiency loss data splitting. Romano, Patterson, Candes (2019). Conformalized quantile regression. NeurIPS 2019. Combines quantile regression conformal calibration adaptive intervals. Romano, Sesia, Candes (2020). Classification valid adaptive coverage. NeurIPS 2020. Introduces Adaptive Prediction Sets (APS) classification. Angelopoulos, Bates, Malik, Jordan (2021). Uncertainty sets image classifiers using conformal prediction. ICLR 2021. Introduces Regularized APS (RAPS) reduce set sizes. Sadinle, Lei, Wasserman (2019). Least ambiguous set-valued classifiers bounded error levels. Journal American Statistical Association, 114(525), 223–234. LAC method classification. Tibshirani, Barber, Candes, Ramdas (2019). Conformal prediction covariate shift. NeurIPS 2019. Weighted conformal prediction distribution shift. Gibbs, Candes (2021). Adaptive conformal inference distribution shift. NeurIPS 2021. Online alpha adjustment sequential prediction. accessible introduction field, see Angelopoulos Bates (2023), Gentle Introduction Conformal Prediction Distribution-Free Uncertainty Quantification.

code: set.seed(42) n <- 300 x <- matrix(rnorm(n * 4), ncol = 4) y <- factor(sample(c("A", "B", "C"), n, replace = TRUE)) x_new <- matrix(rnorm(50 * 4), ncol = 4)  clf <- make_model(   train_fun = function(x, y) glm(y ~ ., data = data.frame(y = y, x),                                   family = "binomial"),   predict_fun = function(object, x_new) {     df <- as.data.frame(x_new)     names(df) <- paste0("X", seq_len(ncol(x_new)))     p <- predict(object, newdata = df, type = "response")     cbind(A = p / 2, B = p / 2, C = 1 - p)   },   type = "classification" )  # \donttest{ result <- conformal_aps(x, y, model = clf, x_new = x_new) print(result) #>  #> ── Conformal Prediction Sets (Adaptive Prediction Sets) ──────────────────────── #> • Coverage target: "90%" #> • Classes: "A, B, C" #> • Training: 150 | Calibration: 150 | Predictions: 50 #> • Median set size: 3 | Mean set size: 3 # }

title: Conformalized Quantile Regression — conformal_cqr
code: set.seed(42) n <- 200 x <- matrix(rnorm(n * 3), ncol = 3) y <- x[, 1] * 2 + rnorm(n) x_new <- matrix(rnorm(20 * 3), ncol = 3)  # Approximating quantile regression with shifted linear models. # In practice, use quantile regression models, e.g.: #   quantreg::rq(y ~ ., data = df, tau = 0.05) model_lo <- make_model(   train_fun = function(x, y) lm(y ~ ., data = data.frame(y = y, x)),   predict_fun = function(obj, x_new) {     predict(obj, newdata = as.data.frame(x_new)) - 1.5   },   type = "regression" ) model_hi <- make_model(   train_fun = function(x, y) lm(y ~ ., data = data.frame(y = y, x)),   predict_fun = function(obj, x_new) {     predict(obj, newdata = as.data.frame(x_new)) + 1.5   },   type = "regression" )  result <- conformal_cqr(x, y, model_lo, model_hi, x_new = x_new) print(result) #>  #> ── Conformal Prediction Intervals (Conformalized Quantile Regression) ────────── #> • Coverage target: "90%" #> • Training: 100 | Calibration: 100 | Predictions: 20 #> • Conformal quantile: -0.038 #> • Median interval width: 2.924

title: CV+ Conformal Prediction Intervals — conformal_cv
text: Unlike basic CV conformal prediction (computes single quantile CV residuals), CV+ constructs intervals vary per test point. test point, every training observation contributes lower upper value based fold model excluded observation, evaluated test point, plus minus leave-fold-residual observation. interval bounds taken quantiles per-observation values. CV+ theoretical coverage guarantee \(1 - 2\alpha\), \(1 - \alpha\) (Barber et al. 2021, Theorem 2). weaker split conformal's \(1 - \alpha\) guarantee. practice, CV+ coverage typically much closer \(1 - \alpha\).

what: predictset: Conformal Prediction and Uncertainty Quantification — predictset-package
title: predictset: Conformal Prediction and Uncertainty Quantification — predictset-package
text: Implements conformal prediction methods constructing prediction intervals (regression) prediction sets (classification) finite-sample coverage guarantees. Methods include split conformal, 'CV+' 'Jackknife+' (Barber et al. 2021) doi:10.1214/20-AOS1965 , 'Conformalized Quantile Regression' (Romano et al. 2019) doi:10.48550/arXiv.1905.03222 , 'Adaptive Prediction Sets' (Romano, Sesia, Candes 2020) doi:10.48550/arXiv.2006.02544 , 'Regularized Adaptive Prediction Sets' (Angelopoulos et al. 2021) doi:10.48550/arXiv.2009.14193 , Mondrian conformal prediction group-conditional coverage (Vovk, Gammerman, Shafer 2005) doi:10.1007/b106715 , weighted conformal prediction covariate shift (Tibshirani et al. 2019) doi:10.48550/arXiv.1904.06019 , adaptive conformal inference sequential prediction (Gibbs Candes 2021) doi:10.48550/arXiv.2106.00170 . methods distribution-free provide calibrated uncertainty quantification without parametric assumptions. Works model can produce predictions new data, including 'lm', 'glm', 'ranger', 'xgboost', custom user-defined models.
